problem:
\[\begin{aligned}
&\text{Definition: } k \in \mathbf{N}.\\
&\text{Definition: } \alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{R}.\\
&\text{Hypothesis: } \alpha \leq \beta.\\
&\text{Definition: } a = (a_1, \dots, a_k) \in \mathbf{R}^k.\\
&\text{Definition: } p : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} \text{ is defined by } p(t) = a_1 + a_2 t + \cdots + a_k t^{k-1}.\\
&\text{Definition: } S = \{a \in \mathbf{R}^k \mid p(0) = 1,\ |p(t)| \leq 1 \text{ for all } t \in [\alpha, \beta]\}.\\
&\text{Goal: determine whether } S \text{ is convex.}
\end{aligned}\]

Definition: $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$.\\ Definition: for a scalar $\alpha$, $\alpha C = \{\alpha x \mid x \in C\}$.\\ Definition: for scalars $\alpha, \beta$, $\alpha C + \beta C = \{u+v \mid u \in \alpha C,\ v \in \beta C\}$.\\ Hypothesis: $\alpha, \beta$ are nonnegative scalars.\\ Goal: prove that $C$ is convex if and only if $(\alpha + \beta)C = \alpha C + \beta C$ for all nonnegative scalars $\alpha, \beta$.

\[\begin{aligned}
&\text{Definition: } A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n},\ b \in \mathbf{R}^m,\ c \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ d \in \mathbf{R}.\\
&\text{Definition: } E \in \mathbf{R}^{p \times m},\ f \in \mathbf{R}^p,\ g \in \mathbf{R}^m,\ h \in \mathbf{R}.\\
&\text{Definition: } \phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m,\quad \phi(x) = \frac{Ax + b}{c^T x + d}.\\
&\text{Definition: } \psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p,\quad \psi(y) = \frac{Ey + f}{g^T y + h}.\\
&\text{Definition: } \mathbf{dom}\,\phi = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\}.\\
&\text{Definition: } \mathbf{dom}\,\psi = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^m \mid g^T y + h > 0\}.\\
&\text{Definition: } M_\phi = \begin{bmatrix} A & b \\ c^T & d \end{bmatrix}.\\
&\text{Definition: } M_\psi = \begin{bmatrix} E & f \\ g^T & h \end{bmatrix}.\\
&\text{Definition: } \Gamma = \psi \circ \phi.\\
&\text{Definition: } \mathbf{dom}\,\Gamma = \{x \in \mathbf{dom}\,\phi \mid \phi(x) \in \mathbf{dom}\,\psi\}.\\
&\text{Goal: prove that } \Gamma \text{ is linear-fractional.}\\
&\text{Goal: prove that the matrix associated with } \Gamma \text{ is } M_\psi M_\phi =
\begin{bmatrix} E & f \\ g^T & h \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} A & b \\ c^T & d \end{bmatrix}. 
\end{aligned}\]

Definition: $K_{\exp} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^3$.\\ Definition: $K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid y > 0,\ y e^{x/y} \le z\}$.\\ Definition: $K_{\exp}^* = \{u \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid u^T v \ge 0 \text{ for all } v \in K_{\exp}\}$.\\ Definition: the closure of $K_{\exp}$ is obtained by adding the points $\{(x,y,z) \mid x \le 0,\ y = 0,\ z \ge 0\}$.\\ Hypothesis: boundary issues may be ignored.\\ Hypothesis: the dual of a cone is equal to the dual of its closure.\\ Goal: determine $K_{\exp}^*$.

Definition: $C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$.\\ Hypothesis: $C$ and $D$ are closed convex cones.\\ Definition: for any cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, $K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}$.\\ Definition: $C^* + D^* = \{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$.\\ Goal: prove that $C \cap D$ is a convex cone.\\ Goal: prove that $C^* + D^*$ is a convex cone.

Definition: $C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Hypothesis: $C$ and $D$ are closed convex cones.\\
Definition: for any cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, $K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}$.\\
Definition: $C^* + D^* = \{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$.\\
Goal: prove that $(C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*$.

Definition: $C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$.\\ Definition: $C$ and $D$ are closed convex cones.\\ Definition: for any cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, $K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}$.\\ Definition: $C^* + D^* = \{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$.\\ Hypothesis: for every closed convex cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, $K^{**} = K$.\\ Goal: prove $(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^*$.\\ Goal: first prove that $(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \Longleftrightarrow C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*$.\\ Goal: then prove $C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*$.\\ Goal: conclude that $(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*$.

\[\begin{array}{l}
\text{Definition: } A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}. \\
\text{Definition: } V = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid Ax \succeq 0\}. \\
\text{Definition: } Ax \succeq 0 \text{ means that each component of } Ax \text{ is nonnegative.} \\
\text{Definition: } \{A^T v \mid v \succeq 0\} \text{ is a subset of } \mathbf{R}^n \text{ with } v \in \mathbf{R}^m. \\
\text{Definition: } v \succeq 0 \text{ means that each component of } v \text{ is nonnegative.} \\
\text{Goal: prove that } V^* = \{A^T v \mid v \succeq 0\}. 
\end{array}\]

\[\text{Definition: } C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } C^\circ \text{ is convex.}\]

Definition: $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$.\\ Definition: The polar set of $C$ is $C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}$.\\ Definition: $C$ is a cone if $\theta x \in C$ for all $x \in C$ and all $\theta \ge 0$.\\ Hypothesis: $C$ is a cone.\\ Goal: determine the polar set $C^\circ$.

Definition: $\|\cdot\|$ is a norm on $\mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $B = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \|x\| \leq 1\}$.\\
Definition: For any set $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, its polar set is $C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}$.\\
Goal: determine the polar set $B^\circ$.

\[\text{Definition: } \mathbf{1} \in \mathbf{R}^n \text{ is the all-ones vector.}\]
\[\text{Definition: } C = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\ x \succeq 0\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\]
\[\text{Goal: determine } C^\circ.\]

\[\text{Definition: } C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } C \text{ is closed.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } C \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } 0 \in C.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } (C^\circ)^\circ = C.\]
Definition: $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$.\\ Definition: $K = \{0\}$.\\ Definition: $K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}$.\\ Goal: describe the dual cone $K^*$.
Definition: $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$.\\ Definition: $K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}$.\\ Hypothesis: $K = \mathbf{R}^2$.\\ Goal: describe the dual cone $K^*$.

\[
\text{Definition: } K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2 \text{ is given by } K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid |x_1| \le x_2\}.
\]
\[
\text{Definition: } K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
\[
\text{Goal: describe } K^*.
\]

\[\text{Definition: } K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2 \text{ is given by } K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.\]
\[\text{Goal: describe } K^*.\]

\[\text{Definition: } v_1,\ldots,v_k \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } P = \operatorname{conv}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Goal: } \sup_{x \in P} f(x) = \max_{i=1,\ldots,k} f(v_i).\]

\[\begin{array}{l}
\text{Definition: } h : \mathbf{R}^k \to \mathbf{R}. \\
\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is convex.} \\
\text{Hypothesis: } \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}^k. \\
\text{Definition: For each } i \in \{1,\dots,k\},\ g_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}. \\
\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \text{ is defined by } f(x) = h(g_1(x), g_2(x), \ldots, g_k(x)). \\
\text{Hypothesis: For each } i \in \{1,\dots,k\}, \text{ at least one of the following holds.} \\
\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is nondecreasing in the } i\text{th argument, with all other arguments fixed, and } g_i \text{ is convex.} \\
\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is nonincreasing in the } i\text{th argument, with all other arguments fixed, and } g_i \text{ is concave.} \\
\text{Hypothesis: } g_i \text{ is affine.} \\
\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}^n.
\end{array}\]

\[\text{Definition: } u \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ t \in \mathbf{R}_{++}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } g(u,t)=t-(1/t)u^T u.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} f=\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}\mid t>\|u\|_2\}.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } g \text{ is concave on } \operatorname{dom} f.\]
\[\text{Definition: use the convexity of the quadratic-over-linear function.}\]

Definition: $u \in \mathbf{R}^n$ and $t \in \mathbf{R}$.\\
Definition: $g(u,t) = -\log\!\left(t - \frac{1}{t} u^T u\right)$.\\
Definition: $\operatorname{dom} f = \{(u,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > \|u\|_2\}$.\\
Hypothesis: use the result established previously.\\
Goal: prove that $g$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} f$.

\[\text{Definition: } f(x,t) = -\log(t^2 - x^T x).\]
\[\text{Definition: } (x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} f = \{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > \|x\|_2\}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } f(x,t) = -\log(t-\|x\|_2) - \log(t+\|x\|_2).\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: this representation follows from standard convexity-preserving composition rules, using the known convexity of } -\log(\cdot) \text{ and the properties of } t \pm \|x\|_2 \text{ on the domain } t > \|x\|_2.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \operatorname{dom} f.\]

\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]\[\text{Definition: } g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex.}\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } f(x) \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in \operatorname{dom} f.\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } g \text{ is concave.}\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } g(x) > 0 \text{ for all } x \in \operatorname{dom} g.\]\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} h = \operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g.\]\[\text{Definition: } h(x) = \frac{f(x)^2}{g(x)} \text{ for } x \in \operatorname{dom} h.\]\[\text{Goal: prove that } h \text{ is convex.}\]

\[\begin{aligned}&\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\\&\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}^n.\\&\text{Hypothesis: } \operatorname{dom} f = \mathbf{R}^n.\\&\text{Hypothesis: } f(0) \le 0.\\&\text{Definition: } g(x,t) = t f(x/t).\\&\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} g = \{(x,t) \mid t > 0\}.\\&\text{Definition: } x \in \mathbf{R}^n \text{ is fixed}.\\&\text{Goal: prove that the function } t \mapsto t f(x/t) \text{ is nonincreasing on } t > 0.\end{aligned}\]

Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$.\\
Hypothesis: $f$ is convex.\\
Hypothesis: $f(0) \le 0$.\\
Definition: $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$.\\
Hypothesis: $g$ is concave.\\
Hypothesis: for all $x \in \operatorname{dom} g$, $g(x) > 0$.\\
Definition: $h(x) = g(x) f(x / g(x))$.\\
Definition: $\operatorname{dom} h = \{x \in \operatorname{dom} g \mid x / g(x) \in \operatorname{dom} f\}$.\\
Goal: prove that $h$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} h$.

\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex.}\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } f(0) \leq 0.\]\[\text{Definition: } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_k > 0,\ k = 1, \dots, n\}.\]\[\text{Definition: } h : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]\[\text{Definition: } h(x) = \dfrac{x^T x}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}}.\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.\]\[\text{Goal: prove that } h \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.\]

Definition: $X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$ is an $n \times n$ real symmetric positive definite matrix.\\
Definition: $t \in \mathbf{R}_{++}$.\\
Definition: $f : \mathbf{S}_{++}^n \times \mathbf{R}_{++} \to \mathbf{R}$ is given by $f(X,t) = nt \log t - t \log \det X$.\\
Definition: $g : \mathbf{S}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is given by $g(X) = n(\operatorname{tr} X) \log(\operatorname{tr} X) - (\operatorname{tr} X)(\log \det X)$.\\
Definition: if $\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n$ are the eigenvalues of $X$, then $g(X) = n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\left(\log \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i - \sum_{i=1}^n \log \lambda_i\right)$.\\
Goal: prove that $f$ is convex in the pair $(X,t)$ on $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n \times \mathbf{R}_{++}$.\\
Goal: using this, prove that $g$ is convex on $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$.

\[	ext{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^m 	o \mathbf{R}.\]
\[	ext{Hypothesis: } f 	ext{ is convex.}\]
\[	ext{Definition: } A \in \mathbf{R}^{m 	imes n},\ b \in \mathbf{R}^m,\ c \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ d \in \mathbf{R}.\]
\[	ext{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} g = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\}.\]
\[	ext{Definition: } g(x) = (c^T x + d)\, f\!\left( rac{Ax+b}{c^T x+d}ight) 	ext{ for } x \in \operatorname{dom} g.\]
\[	ext{Definition: }  rac{Ax+b}{c^T x+d} 	ext{ is the vector } Ax+b \in \mathbf{R}^m 	ext{ divided by the positive scalar } c^T x+d.\]
\[	ext{Goal: prove that } g 	ext{ is convex on } \operatorname{dom} g.\]

\[\text{Definition: } a,c \in \mathbf{R}^n \text{ and } b,d \in \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x)=\frac{a^T x+b}{c^T x+d}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \operatorname{dom} f \text{ is nonempty.}\]
\[\text{Goal: determine the conditions under which } f \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Goal: determine the conditions under which } f \text{ is quasiconvex.}\]

Definition: $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$.\\
Definition: $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$.\\
Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is given by $f(x) = \dfrac{\|Ax-b\|_2^2}{1-x^T x}$.\\
Definition: $B = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \|x\|_2 < 1\}$.\\
Goal: prove that $f$ is convex on $B$.

\[\text{Definition: } n \in \mathbf{N}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x) = \prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n \in \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \alpha_k \ge 0 \text{ for all } k = 1, \ldots, n.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \sum_{k=1}^n \alpha_k \le 1.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is concave on } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.\]

\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex.}\]\[\text{Definition: } g(x,t) = f(x/t).\]\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} g = \{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid x/t \in \operatorname{dom} f,\ t > 0\}.\]\[\text{Goal: prove that } g \text{ is quasiconvex.}\]

\[\text{Definition: } x = (x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4) \in \mathbf{R}^4.\]
\[\text{Definition: } D = \left\{x \in \mathbf{R}^4 \mid x_4 > 0,\ x_3 - \frac{1}{x_4} > 0,\ x_2 - \frac{1}{x_3 - \frac{1}{x_4}} > 0,\ x_1 - \frac{1}{x_2 - \frac{1}{x_3 - \frac{1}{x_4}}} > 0\right\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : D \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x) = \frac{1}{x_1 - \frac{1}{x_2 - \frac{1}{x_3 - \frac{1}{x_4}}}}.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } D.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is decreasing in each variable on } D.\]

\[	ext{Definition: } n \in \mathbf{N}.\]
\[	ext{Definition: } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[	ext{Definition: The scalar Huber function } f_{\mathrm{hub}} : \mathbf{R} 	o \mathbf{R} 	ext{ is given by } f_{\mathrm{hub}}(t)= egin{cases}(1/2)t^2 & |t| \le 1\ |t|-1/2 & |t|>1.\end{cases}\]
\[	ext{Definition: The circularly symmetric Huber function } f_{\mathrm{cshub}} : \mathbf{R}^n 	o \mathbf{R} 	ext{ is given by } f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|_2)= egin{cases}(1/2)\|x\|_2^2 & \|x\|_2 \le 1\ \|x\|_2-1/2 & \|x\|_2>1.\end{cases}\]
\[	ext{Definition: } \|\cdot\|_2 	ext{ is the Euclidean norm.}\]
\[	ext{Goal: prove that } f_{\mathrm{cshub}} 	ext{ is convex.}\]
\[	ext{Goal: find the conjugate function } f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*.\]

\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \operatorname{dom} f.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n \text{ are scalars}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \lambda_1 > 0.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \lambda_i \leq 0 \text{ for } i = 2, \ldots, n.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \lambda_1 + \cdots + \lambda_n = 1.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f(\lambda_1 x_1 + \cdots + \lambda_n x_n) \geq \lambda_1 f(x_1) + \cdots + \lambda_n f(x_n).\]

\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: A function } h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \text{ is monotone nondecreasing if } h(x) \ge h(y) \text{ whenever } x \ge y, \text{ with inequalities interpreted componentwise.}\]
\[\text{Definition: } g(x) = \inf_{\substack{z \in \mathbf{R}^n \\ z \ge 0}} f(x+z), \quad x \in \mathbf{R}^n, \text{ where } z \ge 0 \text{ is componentwise.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } g(x) > -\infty \text{ for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } g \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } g \text{ is monotone nondecreasing.}\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } g(x) \le f(x) \text{ for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is monotone nondecreasing.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } h(x) \le f(x) \text{ for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } h(x) \le g(x) \text{ for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Goal: conclude that } g \text{ is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of } f.\]

\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: for every orthogonal matrix } Q \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \text{ and every } x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ f(Qx) = f(x).\]
\[\text{Definition: this rotational symmetry is equivalent to saying that } f(x) \text{ depends only on } \|x\|_2.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that there exists } \phi : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} \text{ such that } \operatorname{dom} \phi = \mathbf{R},\ \phi \text{ is convex, } \phi \text{ is nondecreasing, and } f(x) = \phi(\|x\|_2) \text{ for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Goal: also prove the converse: every function of the form } f(x) = \phi(\|x\|_2), \text{ with } \phi \text{ convex and nondecreasing, is rotationally symmetric and convex.}\]

\[\begin{aligned}&\text{Definition: } f_1,\ldots,f_m : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}.\\&\text{Hypothesis: } f_1,\ldots,f_m \text{ are convex.}\\&\text{Definition: } g = f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m.\\&\text{Definition: } g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m) \mid x_1,\ldots,x_m \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\} \text{ for } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\\&\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} g = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid g(x)<+\infty\}.\\&\text{Goal: prove that } g \text{ is convex.}\end{aligned}\]

Definition: $m \in \mathbf{N}$, and for each $i=1,\ldots,m$, $f_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{\infty\}$.\\
Hypothesis: each $f_i$ is convex.\\
Definition: $g = f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m$, where
$$g(x) = \inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m) \mid x_1,\ldots,x_m \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\}.$$\\
Definition: $\operatorname{dom} g = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid g(x) < \infty\}$.\\
Definition: for each $i=1,\ldots,m$, the convex conjugate of $f_i$ is
$$f_i^*(y) = \sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} (y^T x - f_i(x)).$$\\
Definition: the convex conjugate of $g$ is
$$g^*(y) = \sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} (y^T x - g(x)).$$\\
Goal: prove that $g^* = f_1^* + \cdots + f_m^*$.

Definition: $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$.\\ Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}$ is a convex function.\\ Definition: $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is defined by $g(x) = f(Ax)$.\\ Definition: $f^*(u) = \sup_{z \in \mathbf{R}^m} (u^T z - f(z))$.\\ Definition: $g^*(y) = \sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} (y^T x - g(x))$.\\ Definition: $A^\dagger = (A A^T)^{-1} A$.\\ Hypothesis: $\operatorname{rank} A = m$.\\ Goal: prove that for every $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $g^*(y) = f^*((A^\dagger)^T y)$.\\ Goal: prove that $\operatorname{dom}(g^*) = A^T \operatorname{dom}(f^*)$.

Definition: $n \ge 1$.\\
Definition: $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$ are positive real numbers.\\
Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is defined by $f(x) = \prod_{i=1}^n (1 - e^{-x_i})^{\lambda_i}$.\\
Definition: $\operatorname{dom} f = \left\{x \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \mid \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e^{-x_i} \le 1\right\}$.\\
Definition: for $i = 1, \ldots, n$, set $y_i = \lambda_i e^{-x_i}/(1 - e^{-x_i})$ and $z_i = y_i/(1 - e^{-x_i})$.\\
Definition: the Hessian is $\nabla^2 f(x) = f(x)(yy^T - \operatorname{diag}(z))$.\\
Goal: prove that $f$ is concave on $\operatorname{dom} f$.

Definition: $a,b \in \mathbf{R}$.\\
Hypothesis: $a < b$.\\
Definition: for each $x=(x_1,\dots,x_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$, define $p_x : [a,b] \to \mathbf{R}$ by $p_x(t)=x_1+x_2 t+x_3 t^2+\cdots+x_n t^{n-1}$.\\
Definition: define $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by $f(x)=\sup_{t\in[a,b]} p_x(t)-\inf_{t\in[a,b]} p_x(t)$.\\
Goal: prove that $f$ is convex on $\mathbf{R}^n$.

\[\text{Definition: } f_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \text{ for } i=1,\ldots,m.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: each } f_i \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid f_i(x) < 0,\ i=1,\ldots,m\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m e^{-1/f_i(x)} \text{ for } x \in \operatorname{dom} f.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \operatorname{dom} f.\]

\[\text{Definition: } g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} g := \{ z \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid g(z) < +\infty \}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } y \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } g \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } y \in \operatorname{dom} g.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f(x) = \inf_{\alpha > 0} \frac{g(y + \alpha x) - g(y)}{\alpha}, \quad x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\]
\[\text{Definition: the infimum is taken in the extended-real sense.}\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex.}\]

Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$.\\
Definition: $S \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$ and $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $f$ is symmetric, i.e., $f(x) = f(Px)$ for every permutation matrix $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$.\\
Definition: $S$ is doubly stochastic, i.e., all entries of $S$ are nonnegative, and every row sum and every column sum is equal to $1$.\\
Hypothesis: $f$ is convex.\\
Goal: prove that $f(Sx) \leq f(x)$.

Definition: $Y \in S^n$.\\
Definition: $Y = Q \, \operatorname{diag}(\lambda) \, Q^T$, where $Q \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$ is orthogonal and $\lambda = (\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is the vector of eigenvalues of $Y$.\\
Definition: $S \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$ is defined by $S_{ij} = Q_{ij}^2$ for all $i,j$.\\
Goal: prove that $S$ is doubly stochastic.\\
Goal: prove that $\operatorname{diag}(Y) = S\lambda$.

Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$.\\
Hypothesis: $f$ is symmetric, i.e., $f(x) = f(Px)$ for every permutation matrix $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$.\\
Hypothesis: $f$ is convex.\\
Definition: $S^n$ is the space of $n \times n$ real symmetric matrices.\\
Definition: For $X \in S^n$, let $\lambda(X) = (\lambda_1(X), \ldots, \lambda_n(X)) \in \mathbf{R}^n$ be the vector of eigenvalues of $X$.\\
Definition: For any square matrix $A$, let $\operatorname{diag}(A) \in \mathbf{R}^n$ be the vector of diagonal entries of $A$.\\
Definition: $\mathcal{V}$ is the set of $n \times n$ real orthogonal matrices.\\
Hypothesis: $X \in S^n$.\\
Hypothesis: Use the results of parts (a) and (b).\\
Goal: prove that $f(\lambda(X)) = \sup_{V \in \mathcal{V}} f(\operatorname{diag}(V^T X V))$.\\
Goal: deduce that $X \mapsto f(\lambda(X))$ is convex on $S^n$.

Definition: $n \ge 1$ is an integer.\\
Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \times \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is given by $f(X,y) = y^T X^{-1} y$.\\
Definition: $\operatorname{dom} f = \{(X,y) \mid X + X^T \succ 0\}$.\\
Definition: $X \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$ and $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $S^n$ is the set of $n \times n$ real symmetric matrices.\\
Hypothesis: when restricted to $X \in S^n$, the function $f$ is convex.\\
Goal: determine whether $f$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} f$. If it is convex, prove it. If it is not convex, give a simple counterexample.

\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} g \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n \text{ is a convex domain.}\]
\[\text{Definition: } g \text{ is twice differentiable on } \operatorname{dom} g.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f(x) = -\exp(-g(x)) \text{ for } x \in \operatorname{dom} g.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: For every } x \in \operatorname{dom} g,\ \begin{bmatrix} \nabla^2 g(x) & \nabla g(x) \\ \nabla g(x)^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \succeq 0.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \operatorname{dom} g.\]

\[\text{Definition: } A \in \mathbf{R}^{m\times n}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } b \in \mathbf{R}^m.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \|\cdot\| \text{ is a norm on } \mathbf{R}^m.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x) = \max\{\|APx - b\| \mid P \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times n} \text{ is a permutation matrix}\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex.}\]

\[\text{Definition: } g,h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } g \text{ and } h \text{ are convex.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } g \text{ and } h \text{ are bounded below.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \operatorname{dom} g = \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: for } x \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ f(x)=\inf\{\theta g(y)+(1-\theta)h(z) \mid y,z \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ \theta y+(1-\theta)z=x,\ 0 \le \theta \le 1\}.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Goal: describe } \operatorname{epi} f \text{ in terms of } \operatorname{epi} g \text{ and } \operatorname{epi} h.\]

\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R} \mid f(x) < +\infty\}.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex if and only if both of the following hold.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \operatorname{dom} f \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: for all } x,y,z \in \operatorname{dom} f \text{ with } x<y<z,\]
\[
\det
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
x & y & z \\
f(x) & f(y) & f(z)
\end{bmatrix}
\geq 0.
\]

\[
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Definition: } Y, Z \in \mathbf{S}^m.\\
&\text{Hypothesis: } 0 \prec Y \preceq Z.\\
&\text{Goal: prove that } \det Y \leq \det Z.
\end{aligned}
\]

\[\text{Definition: } X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n.\]\[\text{Definition: } X = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \text{ with } X_{11} \in \mathbf{S}^m,\ X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)},\ X_{22} \in \mathbf{S}^{n-m}.\]\[\text{Definition: } Y \in \mathbf{S}^m \text{ is the optimization variable.}\]\[\text{Definition: } \log \det Y^{-1} \text{ has domain } \mathbf{S}_{++}^m.\]\[\text{Definition: consider the optimization problem } \min \ \log \det Y^{-1} \text{ subject to } \begin{bmatrix} Y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \preceq \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix}.\]\[\text{Goal: prove that the solution is } Y = X_{11} - X_{12} X_{22}^{-1} X_{12}^T.\]

Definition: $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times m}$ and $\operatorname{rank}(P)=m$.\\
Definition: Assume $P = \begin{bmatrix} I \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, where $I$ is the $m \times m$ identity matrix.\\
Definition: $X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$.\\
Definition: Partition $X$ conformally with $P$ as $X = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix}$, where $X_{11} \in \mathbf{S}^m$, $X_{22} \in \mathbf{S}^{n-m}$, and $X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)}$.\\
Definition: $f : \mathbf{S}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is given by $f(X) = \log \det \left( X_{11} - X_{12} X_{22}^{-1} X_{12}^T \right)^{-1}$.\\
Definition: $\operatorname{dom} f = \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$.\\
Hypothesis: Use the result of part (b) and the minimization property from page 3-19 of the lecture notes.\\
Goal: prove that $f$ is convex.

\[\text{Definition: } P = \begin{bmatrix} I \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times m}, \text{ where } I \text{ is the } m \times m \text{ identity matrix}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } X = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } X_{11} \in \mathbf{S}^m,\; X_{22} \in \mathbf{S}^{n-m},\; X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)}.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } \left( X_{11} - X_{12} X_{22}^{-1} X_{12}^T \right)^{-1} = P^T X^{-1} P.\]
\[\text{Goal: conclude that the function } f \text{ from part (c) can be written as } f(X) = \log \det \left( P^T X^{-1} P \right).\]

Definition: $X$ is a random variable on $\mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $p$ is the density of $X$ with respect to Lebesgue measure on $\mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Hypothesis: $p$ is log-concave.\\
Definition: $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$.\\
Definition: $Y = g(X)$.\\
Hypothesis: $g$ is affine.\\
Hypothesis: $g$ is not constant.\\
Goal: either give a counterexample to the statement that $Y$ has a log-concave density with respect to Lebesgue measure on $\mathbf{R}$, or prove that this statement is true.

Definition: $X$ is an $\mathbf{R}^n$-valued random variable with density $p : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}_+$ with respect to Lebesgue measure.\\
Hypothesis: $p$ is log-concave on $\mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$.\\
Definition: $Y = g(X)$.\\
Hypothesis: $g$ is convex.\\
Goal: determine whether the function $a \mapsto \mathbf{prob}(Y \leq a)$ is log-concave for $a \in \mathbf{R}$. If true, prove it; if false, give a counterexample.

\[\text{Definition: } X \text{ is a random variable on } \mathbf{R}^n \text{ with a log-concave probability density with respect to Lebesgue measure.}\]
\[\text{Definition: } g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \text{ is measurable, and } Y = g(X).\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } g \text{ is concave.}\]
\[\text{Definition: for } s \in \mathbf{R},\ (s)_+ = \max\{s,0\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: for } a \in \mathbf{R},\ F(a) = \mathbf{E}\big((Y-a)_+\big).\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \mathbf{E}\big((Y-a)_+\big) \text{ exists for all relevant } a.\]
\[\text{Goal: determine whether } F(a) \text{ is convex in } a \in \mathbf{R}, \text{ and whether } F(a) \text{ is log-concave in } a \in \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Goal: either prove the statement or give a counterexample.}\]

\[
\begin{array}{l}
\text{Definition: } a \in \mathbf{R}^n.\\
\text{Definition: } C = \{(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1, 2, \ldots, n)\} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n.\\
\text{Definition: } y \in \mathbf{R}^n.\\
\text{Definition: } S_C(y) = \max_{x \in C} y^T x.\\
\text{Definition: For any vector } u \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ u_{[1]}, u_{[2]}, \ldots, u_{[n]} \text{ are the components of } u \text{ arranged in nonincreasing order.}\\
\text{Goal: prove that } S_C(y) = a_{[1]}y_{[1]} + a_{[2]}y_{[2]} + \cdots + a_{[n]}y_{[n]}.
\end{array}
\]

Definition: $a \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $C \subset \mathbf{R}^n$ is the set of all permutations of $a$, i.e. $C = \{(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)$ is a permutation of $(1,2,\ldots,n)\}$.\\
Definition: $S_C(y) = \sup\{y^T z \mid z \in C\}$ for $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: For $u \in \mathbf{R}^n$, let $u_{[1]} \ge u_{[2]} \ge \cdots \ge u_{[n]}$ be the components of $u$ sorted in nonincreasing order.\\
Definition: For $k = 1, \ldots, n$, define $s_k(u) = u_{[1]} + u_{[2]} + \cdots + u_{[k]}$.\\
Definition: $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Goal: prove that the condition $x^T y \le S_C(y)$ for all $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$ holds if and only if $s_k(x) \le s_k(a)$ for $k = 1, \ldots, n-1$, and $s_n(x) = s_n(a)$.\\
Definition: When these inequalities hold, $a$ majorizes $x$.

Definition: $a \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $C = \{(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1, 2, \ldots, n)\} \subset \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: The support function of $C$ is $S_C(y) = \sup_{z \in C} y^T z$.\\
Definition: The convex conjugate of $S_C$ is $S_C^*(x) = \sup_{y \in \mathbf{R}^n} (x^T y - S_C(y))$.\\
Definition: For $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, say that $x$ is majorized by $a$ if, after rearranging the components of $x$ and $a$ in nonincreasing order, one has $\sum_{i=1}^k x_{[i]} \le \sum_{i=1}^k a_{[i]}$ for $k = 1, \ldots, n-1$, and $\sum_{i=1}^n x_i = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i$.\\
Goal: prove that
$$
S_C^*(x) =
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } x \text{ is majorized by } a,\\
+\infty & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$
\\
Goal: conclude that $x$ is a convex combination of the permutations of $a$ if and only if $a$ majorizes $x$.

\[\begin{array}{l}\text{Definition: } n=2.\\\text{Definition: } \theta \in \mathbf{R}^2.\\\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^2 \to \mathbf{R},\quad f(x)=x_1^{\theta_1}x_2^{\theta_2}.\\\text{Definition: } x \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^2.\\\text{Hypothesis: } \theta \succeq 0.\\\text{Hypothesis: } 1^T\theta = 1.\\\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is concave on } \mathbf{R}_{++}^2.\end{array}\]

Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$.\\
Definition: for $x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n$ and $\theta = (\theta_1, \dots, \theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n}$.\\
Hypothesis: $\theta \succeq 0$.\\
Definition: $\theta \succeq 0$ means $\theta_i \ge 0$ for all $i = 1, \dots, n$.\\
Hypothesis: $1^T \theta = 1$.\\
Goal: prove that $f$ is concave on $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$.

Definition: $n \geq 1$.\\
Definition: $\theta = (\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0,\ i=1,\ldots,n\}$.\\
Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is defined by $f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n}$.\\
Definition: $1 \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is the all-ones vector.\\
Hypothesis: $\theta \succeq 0$, i.e. $\theta_i \geq 0$ for all $i$.\\
Hypothesis: $1^T \theta \leq 1$.\\
Goal: prove that $f$ is concave on $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$.

\[\text{Definition: } n \in \mathbf{N}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \theta = (\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0,\ i = 1,\dots,n\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } \theta \preceq 0.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \theta \preceq 0 \text{ means } \theta_i \le 0 \text{ for all } i = 1,\dots,n.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.\]

\[\begin{array}{l}
\text{Definition: } n \geq 1. \\
\text{Definition: } \theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n. \\
\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n}. \\
\text{Definition: } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0 \text{ for } i = 1, \ldots, n\}. \\
\text{Hypothesis: } 1^T \theta = 1. \\
\text{Hypothesis: Exactly one component of } \theta \text{ is positive.} \\
\text{Hypothesis: Equivalently, there exists exactly one index } i \text{ such that } \theta_i > 0, \text{ and } \theta_j \leq 0 \text{ for all } j \neq i. \\
\text{Goal: Prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.
\end{array}\]

\[\text{Definition: } n \in \mathbf{N}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \theta = (\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0,\ i = 1,\dots,n\}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } 1^T \theta \ge 1.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: There exists a unique index } i \text{ such that } \theta_i > 0.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: For all } j \ne i,\ \theta_j \le 0.\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.\]

\[
\text{Definition: } f,g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}.
\]
\[
\text{Definition: } f(x) = \|x\|_1, \quad g(x) = \frac{1}{2}\|x\|_2^2.
\]
\[
\text{Definition: } h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \text{ is the infimal convolution of } f \text{ and } g, \text{ given by } h(x) = \inf_{y \in \mathbf{R}^n} \left(f(y) + g(x-y)\right).
\]
\[
\text{Definition: } h(x) = \inf_{y \in \mathbf{R}^n} \left(\|y\|_1 + \frac{1}{2}\|x-y\|_2^2\right).
\]
\[
\text{Definition: } \phi : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}, \quad \phi(u) = \begin{cases} u^2/2, & |u| \leq 1,\\ |u|-1/2, & |u| > 1. \end{cases}
\]
\[
\text{Definition: } x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n.
\]
\[
\text{Goal: prove that for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n, \quad h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n \phi(x_i).
\]

\[\text{Definition: } n \ge 1.\]
\[\text{Definition: } h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Definition: } \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}.\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is convex.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is nondecreasing.}\]
\[\text{Hypothesis: } h(t) = h(0) \text{ for all } t \le 0.\]
\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \quad f(x) = h(\|x\|_2).\]
\[\text{Goal: prove that } f \text{ is convex on } \mathbf{R}^n.\]

\[\text{Definition: } h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}.\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is convex.}\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } h \text{ is nondecreasing.}\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}.\]\[\text{Hypothesis: } h(t) = h(0) \text{ for all } t \le 0.\]\[\text{Definition: } f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \text{ is defined by } f(x) = h(\|x\|_2).\]\[\text{Definition: } y \in \mathbf{R}^n.\]\[\text{Goal: prove that } f^*(y) = h^*(\|y\|_2).\]

Definition: $p > 1$.\\
Definition: $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Definition: $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is given by $f(x) = (1/p)\|x\|_2^p$.\\
Definition: $h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}$ is given by $h(t) = \frac{1}{p}\max\{0,t\}^p = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{p} t^p & t \ge 0,\\ 0 & t < 0. \end{cases}$\\
Definition: for part (b), if $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is defined by $g(x) = h(\|x\|_2)$, then $g^*(y) = h^*(\|y\|_2)$ for all $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$.\\
Goal: derive the convex conjugate $f^*$ by applying the result of part (b) to the above function $h$.
